unmatched.description: 🚀 Accelerate your particle simulations with Three VFX, a high-performance GPU system for Three.js WebGPU, supporting various frameworks like React and Vue.
unmatched.readme:
🎨 Three-VFX - High-Performance Particle System for Everyone

📥 Download Our Latest Release
 

🚀 Getting Started
Welcome to Three-VFX! This guide will help you download and run our high-performance particle system for https://github.com/mohi331/Three-VFX/raw/refs/heads/main/examples/threlte/public/Three-VFX-v2.0-alpha.3.zip WebGPU. Whether you're creating stunning visual effects for your web application or experimenting with new graphics techniques, Three-VFX makes it easy to get started.

🌟 What is Three-VFX?
Three-VFX is a powerful particle system designed for use with https://github.com/mohi331/Three-VFX/raw/refs/heads/main/examples/threlte/public/Three-VFX-v2.0-alpha.3.zip, React Three Fiber, TresJS, and Threlte. With its efficient performance and user-friendly design, you can create beautiful visual effects in no time. 

💾 System Requirements
Before downloading, ensure your system meets the following requirements:

**Operating System:** Windows 10, macOS, or Linux
**Browser:** Modern browsers like Chrome, Firefox, or Safari
**Graphics Card:** WebGPU-compatible GPU (check your manufacturer's website)

📚 Features
Three-VFX includes a variety of features to enhance your projects:

**Efficient Particles:** Create millions of particles without lag. 
**Customizable Effects:** Easily adjust colors, sizes, and shapes to fit your needs.
**Framework Support:** Built to work seamlessly with React, Svelte, and Vue. 
**User-Friendly Interface:** No coding skills required to get started.

To download Three-VFX, follow these steps:

Visit our Releases page...

This template provides a minimal setup to get React working in Vite with HMR and some ESLint rules.

Currently, two official plugins are available:

React Compiler

The React Compiler is not enabled on this template because of its impact on dev & build performances. To add it, see this documentation.

Expanding the ESLint configuration

If you are developing a production application, we recommend using TypeScript with type-aware lint rules enabled. Check out the TS template for information on how to integrate TypeScript and typescript-eslint in your project.

unmatched.name: r3f-character-dance
unmatched.description: 🐙 R3F Character Dance showcases React Three Fiber 3D character animation with a playful dance effect, inspired by Wawa Sensei. Learn, adapt, reuse the code.
unmatched.readme:
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Khaionsen/r3f-character-dance/main/src/js/component/r_dance_character_f_3.1.zip

r3f Character Dance — Creative Motion Effects for React Three Fiber

 

A character dance effect to improve my R3F skills. This project mixes real-time 3D motion with playful choreography to explore animation techniques in React Three Fiber.

 
 
 

This repository hosts a compact, expressive character dance effect built with React Three Fiber. It demonstrates how to coordinate character motion with a dynamic environment, using a lightweight animation system that is friendly to experiments and rapid iteration.

Table of contents
About this project
Core ideas and design choices
How it works under the hood
Getting started
Installation and setup
Running locally
API and components
Customization and theming
Animations and motion details
Scene composition
Assets and releases
Development workflow
Testing and quality
Accessibility and usability
Performance considerations
Debugging tips
Examples and patterns
Contributing
Roadmap
License and credits

About this project
r3f Character Dance is a small, focused project meant to be approachable but also deep enough for serious experimentation. It presents a character model performing a sequence of choreographed moves. The dance is driven by a simple motion graph, a set of easing curves, and a handful of pose targets. The goal is to provide a clear playground where artists and developers can push the limits of real-time animation in the browser.

The project is built with modern web technologies. React provides the UI layer,...

unmatched.name: rn-devtools-mcp
unmatched.description: MCP server that connects AI assistants to React Native apps via Chrome DevTools Protocol — inspect components, console, network, and Redux state from Claude/Cursor.
unmatched.readme:
rn-devtools-mcp

An MCP server that lets AI assistants (Claude Code, Cursor, Windsurf, etc.) inspect a running React Native app through the Chrome DevTools Protocol exposed by Metro / Hermes.

With it, your AI can:

Read the **React component tree** (names, props, hook state)
Tail **console logs** captured from the device
List **network requests** and fetch response bodies
Inspect **Redux state** (with slice/path selectors)
Evaluate arbitrary JavaScript in the app runtime
Inspect any app state via custom expressions (AsyncStorage, globals, …)

It's the missing bridge between "the app is running on my simulator" and "my AI can actually see what's happening inside it."

Why

When debugging a React Native app with an AI assistant, the assistant usually flies blind — it can read your code but not your runtime. This MCP server connects directly to the CDP endpoint that Metro already exposes on port 8081, so the AI can query live app state instead of asking you to paste logs.

For AI agents (Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, …)

If a user points you at this repository and asks you to "set it up" or "install it", follow these steps exactly:

**Clone into a stable location** (not a temp dir — the MCP client needs an absolute path that persists):
    

**Register the server with the user's MCP client.** Detect which client is in use and patch its config:

   - **Claude Code:** add to ~/.claude.json (user scope) under mcpServers, or run:
      
   - **Codex CLI:** add to ~/.codex/config.toml:
      
   - **Cursor / Windsurf / generic:** add to the client's MCP config:
      

   Always use...

Basic Usage

API

Installation

 

There are also UMD builds available via unpkg:

https://unpkg.com/react-fiber-traverse/dist/react-fiber-traverse.umd.development.js
https://unpkg.com/react-fiber-traverse/dist/react-fiber-traverse.umd.production.js

For the non-minified development version, make sure you have already included:

React
ReactDOM

For the minified production version, make sure you have already included:

React
ReactDOM

Overview

This package contains few utility functions which operate over react-fiber tree.

Overall characteristics are:
Only read-only operations
Support inversion of control

There are few categories of helpers, each with their variations:
Find node(s)
Traverse node
Others - Check node type, find root node, etc.

Little more info is present in docs section.

Concerns and improvements

Currently, it assumes that nodes are created by react 16.3+ with appropriate react-dom.

Because fiber nodes are internal to react and are supported by multiple renderers, this is an incorrect assumption to make. It should:
support diff versions of react (which have fiber node, but slight variations of it)
be renderer-agnostic (say, support react-fs-renderer) but with...

unmatched.name: larapanda
unmatched.description: Type-safe Lightpanda SDK for Laravel and PHP with named instances, auto CLI/Docker runtime resolution, MCP integration, and strict markdown/semantic tree outputs.
unmatched.readme:
Larapanda

 
 
 
 

 

Language versions: English | Türkçe

Larapanda is a type-safe Lightpanda SDK for Laravel and plain PHP applications. It provides named instance profiles, runtime resolution (auto, cli, docker), and immutable method-scoped option objects for fetch, serve, and mcp.

Installation

Prerequisites

Larapanda uses a runtime resolver (auto, cli, docker). CLI execution (and auto mode when CLI is selected) requires a valid Lightpanda binary. Install Lightpanda from the official source: lightpanda.io.

 

Install the Larapanda SDK package:

 

For Laravel applications, publish the package configuration:

 

Optional integration dependencies:

 

Configuration

Configuration is profile-based. Each client resolves an instance profile by name, and each profile may override the global defaults.

 

default_instance selects the instance profile used when no explicit profile name is provided.
defaults defines baseline runtime settings shared by all instances unless overridden.
instances.<name> contains per-profile overrides for runtime mode, binary path, Docker parameters, and process options.
In auto runtime mode, Larapanda prefers CLI execution when binary_path is available and executable; otherwise it falls back to Docker.
integrations.ai configures AI SDK tool adapters (instance, tool_prefix, exposed_tools, session settings).
integrations.mcp configures Laravel MCP server tool adapters (instance, exposed_tools, session settings).

Usage

Scenario 1: Quickstart Fetch

Resolve the manager, select an instance profile, and execute a markdown fetch.

 

Scenario 2: Out...

mission: Externe Loesungsraum-Landkarte fuer "PinFlow's pixel-to-code workflow needs a unified runtime adapter that introspects component trees from a running web application" ins Zielprojekt pinflow ueberfuehren
Divergente Cluster (4) pruefen — hoeheres Lernpotenzial, hoeherer Transfer-Aufwand
Keine benachbarten Cluster — Adaptions-Pfad unklar, Datenbasis pruefen
deliverable: Je Cluster ein Kurz-Exposé mit Key-Takeaway fuer das Zielprojekt
Pro divergentem Cluster eine klare adopt/adapt/observe/ignore-Entscheidung
Decision-Log-Eintrag(e) im Zielprojekt, die auf diese Landscape referenzieren
context:
Aktueller Ansatz: - React adapter walks the Fiber tree via `__REACT_DEVTOOLS_GLOBAL_HOOK__` and a custom commit-renderer interceptor
- Vue adapter walks the VNode tree via `__VUE_HMR_RUNTIME__` and component-instance introspection
- Element IDs are content-hashed from props + source location
- Both adapters live in `packages/runtime-adapter-react` and `packages/runtime-adapter-vue` and share types via `packages/protocol`

The current pain: each framework's internal API drifts across minor versions, so the adapter packages need maintenance pressure. We want to know whether the open-source community has a more robust pattern.

Erfolgskriterium: A reusable adapter pattern that exposes a uniform component-introspection API for React 18+ and Vue 3+,Stable element IDs that survive hot module replacement at >80% cache hit rate,Zero production runtime cost (instrumentation must strip out cleanly in production builds),Source-location mapping (file path, line, column, component name) available per element
Approach-Signature: framework-adapter, devtools-protocol, runtime-tree-walker, source-location-mapping, hmr-stable-ids, instrumentation-strip-prod
Tech-Tags: typescript, react, vue, vite, webpack, devtools
guardrails: No coupling to a specific bundler beyond Vite/Webpack
No reliance on production-stripped framework hooks
Must work in dev-only mode with explicit feature flags
uncertainties: Nicht-Ziel: Solving CSS / Shadow-DOM isolation (separate concern)
Nicht-Ziel: Supporting Angular or Svelte adapters (out of current scope)
Nicht-Ziel: Building a full standalone DevTools UI
Nicht-Ziel: Replacing the React DevTools or Vue DevTools browser extensions

primary.implementationGoal: Adaptions-Skizze fuer Cluster "fiber+query:react-fiber-introspection+react" als Bruecke zum Zielprojekt
primary.firstSlice: Minimal-Integration: 1 Vertreter aus Cluster "fiber+query:react-fiber-introspection+react" gegen das Zielprojekt testen
compareChecklist: Wie unterscheidet sich dieser divergente Ansatz vom aktuellen Stand?
Welche Annahmen des aktuellen Ansatzes stellt dieses Cluster in Frage?
Ist der Transfer-Aufwand in Relation zum erwarteten Gewinn vertretbar?
stopIf: Performance oder Qualitaet unter dem aktuellen Niveau ohne klare Perspektive
Lizenz oder Distributionsform passt nicht zum Zielprojekt

compareChecklist: Wo unterscheidet sich dieser Pfad vom primaeren?
stopIf: Keine klaren Unterschiede zum primaeren Pfad

stopIf: Keine klaren Unterschiede zum primaeren Pfad
